speaker: interviewer
text: --6th of December, 1995, interview of Eva Hoffman, tape number 3. You were saying--

speaker: interviewee
text: --that we arrived to Belsen-Bergen. And they made us undress, and were taking us in a bath through the street in the nude.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a no, no person, like a nobody, nothing, a nobody. It's humiliating, completely nude and naked. Street-- there were people on the street walking by. But nobody even paid any attention to us, a bunch of naked girls. They took us, like, 50, 100 at a time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave us a shower. About how far? I would say about a block and a half. And then we returned.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is in winter?

speaker: interviewee
text: In winter, January, the 4th or the 5th of January--

speaker: interviewee
text: --1945. And they gave us a shower. And then we returned. They let us take some clothes. For instance, you take one dress and you take one coat. The coat consisted of the striped uniform, which one is famous from the camps. And the dress, I ended up with a Tyrolean dress, which one eventually lost the stitches. And I was exposed in the most undesirable places. And no underwear-- I was lucky I saved an-- a pair of underpants. I don't remember how. And everybody had their own shoes. And I had two pair of socks in the shoes. I couldn't put on the shoes exactly. But I took off the one pair of socks. I used it for mittens. And then I had one pair of wool socks with my custom-made, high-laced winter shoes, which one gave me two times an extra portion of food, since I sold it to an overseer or somebody of means. And since the shoes was size 35, not too many people could wear it. So they gave it back to me. But I had eaten the meal already. I discarded the shoes in Sweden many years after the war. They was just as durable as I was. I had nails in from the time of the cleaning of the ruins. I put nails in the soles for less wear on the soles. So that's how I had the shoes survive so well. And took us from there in a different location. We walked again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Still in Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Still in Bergen-Belsen. We walked somewhere. And they put us, a thousand girls, in a barrack. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Should we say we had more or less standing-room only? That's how tightly we were packed. Eventually, we straightened out a little bit. We laid down a little bit. There were some people who weren't from our group. They came-- they were coming from somewhere else. So one day, they came and-- work force. So we went work force, carrying some, loading [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from one barrack to another barrack. But they-- you carried it, you know, like, in your arms, just. They weren't packed or anything. You just picked up a heap of clothing, and you carried home what you could. And there, when-- I met one of my school friends. She was there. She started to holler Eva, Eva! She was there with her family. She was from the group from not far from Budapest, which one was transported with the whole families. It was like a family camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she was there with her mother and father and brother. I don't remember what was the family name, Herzog or Hertzberg, something. But I never know whatever happened to them, if any of them survived. This was the only time I seen them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we ended up in a-- and again, another time of a shower. A few days, a few weeks later-- time was not of a big essence in those days-- transported us to our block number 208. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there where I was the whole time, didn't do any kind of a work except went to pick up the food till I was able. And some reason after a while, I wasn't able to carry, didn't realize why. But we were not doing anything. We stood on Appell once in a while. We were just--

speaker: interviewer
text: It wasn't every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever get ill that you needed--

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: --to go to a doctor?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't really ill. I-- when other people had dysentery, I was constipated. And when other people were all right, I got diarrhea. And one morning, I was running out. And this is just a private incident. We had-- the latrines were digged. And by our latrine, there was a big circle, a hole, after the latrine that had some clothing buried there some years ago. And the ground just fell in.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the latrines were made like a long rod, like you sit on, on two sides. And this gaping hole was next to the latrine. But it was in conjunction with the latrine. So one morning I came out. And in my weakened state, one of the Russian woman, who was the overseer of it, she went and she gave me one shove with a shovel. And I fell right into the latrine face-first.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And looked like she got scared or what. I had my container-- what you use for food. That is like a big pitcher. And I digged in it, too, very good in the latrine until I got myself somewhat raised. And she handed me, like, the end of the shovel, though, to help me to come out of it. So that's how I came out of the latrine, soaked from top to bottom in the, the best what Belsen-Bergen had to offer. And one of the girls from the barrack went to the overseer to ask to let me into the bathroom. The bathrooms were off-limit. Every barrack had two bathrooms on each end. Bathrooms were off-limits. Just people in position, high position-- [NON-ENGLISH], Blockaltestes, they were the only ones who could use it. We weren't allowed to use their bathrooms whatsoever. Bathrooms consisted of two sides, or three sides, long basins made out of cement. And there were, like, faucets over it with running cold water. There was no bathroom facility or anything involved in it. So they didn't want us to have any water.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the girl got a beating account of me, account of she insisted that she wanted to talk to that overseer for my sake. And the woman was hilarious after she beat up the girl. I, I can't remember what was her name, Miriam? Miriam? I don't even remember what was her name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody was out for themselves. You didn't make no lasting friendship with anybody. This was, like, spur of the moment. You knew somebody. And then worse times come about. You are fending for yourself. Sisters parted company. And everybody became like slightly animal, like an animal. Nobody cared for anybody, really. Everybody was just self-preservation. And this girl who got beaten on account of me, till-- it hurts me till today. And this [? way ?] I-- I got washed with the ice-cold water, at least got something off me. And my clothes-- I was in a blanket all day till my clothes dried somewhat. But it wasn't nice somebody has to get the beating on my account. And from there, we got-- we went out periodically for the Zahlappell, but not too often. But the last three weeks was brutal. There wasn't any food whatsoever. And for a while, I was in a stronger condition, was still helping out, getting a little bit of extra, like carrying the food, and to carry out the dead ones who, who died, like, overnight, so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you got extra rations for that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really a ration. Let's say they gave you, like, one spoonful of food. For your extra effort, you get a spoon and a half full of food. But then the food stopped all together. My energy stopped, too. I couldn't do it anymore. I couldn't drag the bodies out. So there was so many of it. We just pulled it outdoors. And nobody had any name to it or anything. They was just thrown away like excess garbage. And one night-- one night we had gotten a, a nice meal, a really nice meal. There was a piece of meat in it. I had two sisters next to us. I was with a girlfriend. This time already-- all the girls, we were together with the-- they got lost in the shuffle. They went to different places. And they got separated from us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So two sisters, they were next to us. And one of them died on a piece of meat. She had a-- You mean choked?

speaker: interviewee
text: She choked to death. She wouldn't give it up. She wouldn't open her mouth. And we couldn't take it out of her mouth. She died right there, front of us, from that little piece of meat. I mean, food was that precious that she wouldn't give it up. She'd rather died on that piece of meat. She choked on it. And then I had another neighbor, she just wanted water. I said, you cannot have no water. She got bloated. She had blisters hanging on her-- what should I tell you-- like a five-pound bags of blisters. That was all to do with malnutrition. And she had typhoid. I had typhoid. My girlfriend had typhoid. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the last three weeks, we didn't get nothing, didn't go nowhere. We didn't get nothing. One nice day, we went out to steal a little bit of potato peel. I was such a big knocker, my head got stuck on the-- on the fence-- on wire fence. So I left the hat behind. And I ran as fast as I could. Now, people-- a lot of people got beating. My girlfriend got a beating-- account of food. And then the, the-- [NON-ENGLISH] came around to look at us. But she had plenty time left to look account of the lines were getting so thin already. You could see through.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she could always find me-- her, with her obsession with my hair. My girlfriend got shaved off account that she had lice. That I had the same amount of lice, she will never forgive me that I got-- she got shaven and I didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the people were dying left and right. And one night, another girl-- there were two sister-in-laws. Everybody was telling about previous lives. So these were two sister-in-laws. And one says-- oh, she was as ugly as sin-- proposed she was a very good-looking young woman before. No hair-- she was fallen, too. She was telling about her wedding. And things went-- so one night, she stretched her arm through my neck. And I went and took the hand and threw it away. And that hand came back again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I turned around. And I says to her, you can't even leave me be? The hand was cold. That's when I realized that, that I was disturbing the dead. She was sharing your bunk with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No bunk. We were laying on the floor. We had no blankets, no nothing. If you wanted to have a pillow underneath you, then you rolled up your coat. And if you wanted to cover yourself, you laid down on that cement-- extremely primitive conditions. We didn't give nothing. They didn't give us nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you deal with the cold?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] God was very, very good to us. They gave us such a cold winter and so much snow that winter. It was an extra punishment for our easy life. [SOBBING] It was frozen, should we say, like, the blood barely went around in you account of it was so cold. We were constantly cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the most difficult thing you had to deal with in the camp that you can think of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the food was something people were talking about. This was an old story. Everybody cooked, even if didn't know how to hold a-- a cooking spoon in their hand. Everybody was cooking. I don't know what was the point. And-- but everybody was cooking. And everybody was starving. This was one of the things, that no food-- and the-- the people who had done wrong, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like the girl who-- whose hand I took away from me when I didn't realize she was dead, and the one who got the beating account of me in the latrine. This was one of the hardest things. For years, it was haunting me. For years, I had bathroom, bathroom dreams, which went like, I had to go. And it was unavailable to go. There was always something which won't-- didn't let me to use the facilities. For years, I had nightmares about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you still have these nightmares? Or they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in that extent. Once in a blue moon, I get some disturbance. But maybe tonight I will have, after living it again. But over the years, the feelings fade. It's not as strong as they used to be years ago. I used to break out crying, just the thought of it that the woman got a beating account of me. She was a young girl. And I have a friend of mine in Canada, which one-- once in a blue moon, very seldom we get together. We-- we were in the same barrack. And not too often, we do make a couple of words of reminiscent about it. People had no faces. People have no names. You didn't know what-- sometimes, you know people that they came from. But you have no names. And after all those years, no names remain.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever feel so completely overcome with despair that you, like, didn't think that you could carry on?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. There was, like the German says, [GERMAN], it doesn't really matter. This was the general thought about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what helped you to survive, to keep going, even though you were hungry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Que sera, sera, whatever gonna be, gonna be. Never gave a, a second thought that I could die or I could survive, just went with the flood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any prisoners who had special treatment to other prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Like-- it was, like, a clique of Slovakian girls. They were there a long time, not to begrudge them. They were four years there before. The Hungarians were-- we were princesses. We didn't go through the same torture as they went. So they try to inflict a little bit on us, what we hadn't gotten before. So she-- they wanted to make it a little bit more spicy for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: These are Jewish girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: They decided that-- we had a woman with us from Budapest. She was an, an artist, a painter, or a sculptor, or whatever. She didn't know anything about Jewishness. But she comes from Jewish background. She had bleached blond hair.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: It still was growing out, whatever she had. Was-- it was-- looked like she was blond to begin with. But she helped herself a little bit. They were making fun of her. They were chopping her hair off in the most funniest way, you know, like-- not like they're giving a haircut, like making a rag doll out of [INAUDIBLE]. They sort of were chopping her hair off.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of an actor-- what kind of an art she know how to do? If she said that she was a sculptor or a painter, they were laughing at her. I mean, they're making, really, a, a scapegoat out of her.

speaker: interviewee
text: People who had the special talents, like, ones that decide that they're going to make a show for us. We're going to do the show ourselves. We're looking for volunteers. You have too big hand. No, you have too big feet. They are making, you know, up extremely unpleasant remarks. And the people were very vulnerable, all of us, account of we were cold and hungry and without parents, without hope, without any kind of a backbone. You know, no, this, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this was the only attitude you could take. Whatever gonna be, gonna be.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you realize that the war was coming to an end? What happened? Did--

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing I realized something was wrong-- for three weeks, we didn't get not a drop of food or a drink of water. We got as bold as went outside. We had a reservoir next to our barrack. And we tried to tie a strings from whatever. I don't remember from what we made the strings. Maybe-- we, we haven't got no scissors or anything-- to get a little bit of water out of the green reservoir with the moss on it and all that. People were that starved. They wanted to get-- naturally, whoever got the water out of there, they all died. The water was contaminated. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Maybe any other time, they wouldn't have been as bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you know that the war was coming to an end?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I didn't know nothing of that sort. But the-- when the freedom came, they came very surprisingly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what happened? And when was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- this was around the days when we were trying to get water out of the reservoir. The weather was nice already. And we weren't that frozen. So went out a little bit to get a little sunshine. And we didn't wait that they should let us out. We wandered out. Whatever gonna happen, gonna happen. Usually, we couldn't go out in the daytime if there wasn't an Appell or something. But went out and sat down by the reservoir.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all of a sudden, some people came from another barrack. Did you hear? What did we hear? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That the doors are open. What doors? The gates between the man and the woman's lager, they were open.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's how we came to know about it, nothing more. A couple of shots were fired here and there. But that didn't interest us whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't see any SS people around?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, they came around. But that was an everyday happening, they came around. They didn't wear anything different then they wore any other day. And then all of a sudden, somebody went-- one of the bolder ones-- went all the way to the kitchen and says that the gates are open between the men's and the women's lager. That's how we know. We were in such an apathy already, you know, no thought left account of you need food for the-- my mind to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we had nothing whatsoever for three weeks. And we weren't very well-fed from before. And our mind was not functioning so very well.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we-- my girlfriend decided we should go and organize. I said, what should we organize? I said, I can't even walk. So we went and organized on the men's side. I found an [NON-ENGLISH] coat.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- probably I could wrap myself three times down to the floor. But it was better than the striped one what I had. The dress fell apart. They had a V in the front. The stitches fell out. So I had to tie a knot not to be exposed. I mean, I looked ridiculous like this. My girlfriend had a pink dress with an organza inset and nothing underneath it. I mean, this was another very lovely dress.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I got-- I put on the coat. And I had my own shoes. But the only thing what we found is shoe paste. It wasn't a very big number. Nobody ended up in-- [INAUDIBLE]. Of storage room, something-- shoe paste was there by the millions. There where I found the coat. A little bit of shoe paste was on it, but who cares? It was a nice warm coat.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I found a wallet and one Hungarian pengo in, in the pile, whatever. And I slit a wallet, a man's, and a pengo was in it. And another day, I walked myself around with the [NON-ENGLISH] coat. And I am walking. And I'm-- went into a warehouse-- was, like, somewhat under the ground-- had sour pickle and cabbage and things like that in tremendous barrels, each barrel as big as a room. And a couple of people fell in there. But the brine or whatever didn't take too long to kill them. And I don't know how-- somebody was digging some cabbage out of a, a barrel. And I says to him, I want some, too. So he gave me some, in about-- in a bowl, what, something I had. And as I was walking with my big deal, with that little bit of sour cabbage, a man calls me by my name. And I am looking at him. He had, like, a ski cap with the points. [POINTS] I didn't recognize him who he was. And I didn't think about it till today. I, I never knew who the man was to know me by my name. And here comes a Russian woman, and-- who says to me in Russian that I don't need it-- took away the cabbage from me. This was the end of my escapade. But again, I made a mistake. I didn't think why or who the man was. I was so happy with my little bit of cabbage.
